content.full_text:
Fern PolIrIcel PRACTICEs CouulSSIoN
428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916;) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
September 11,2006
Dr. Londres Uso
31302 Via Las Palmas
San Juan Capistrano, CA 92675
R-E: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No.I-06-163
Dear Dr. Uso:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").l Because your request seeks general guidance, we are
treatingyour request as one for informal assistance.2 Please note that although this letter
concerns itself with certain incidents ofpast conduct, it is not being offered as an
evaluation of that conduct, but rather as advice regarding your future reporting
obligations. (See regulation 18329(c)@)(A) and Chtn Advice Letter, No. 4-05-086.)
QUESTIONS
1. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you, a candidate for city
council, from writing a column for the local newspaper?
2. Is the publication of your column by a newspaper without any charge to you a
reportable contribution to you from the newspaper?
CONCLUSIONS
1. The Act does not prohibit you from writing a column for the local newspaper
during your candidacy for office. If elected, writing the column without compensation
does not, in itself violate the Act's conflict-of-interest rules.
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - 9l}l4. Commission regulations apper at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83 I 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)


File No. I-06-163
Page No. 2
2. The free publication of your column containing references to your candidacy
by the local newspaper may be a reportable contribution unless the newspaper offers
equal opporfunity and treatment for all candidates for the same office.
FACTS
You are a candidate for City Council in San Juan Capistrano. The city clerk has
suggested that you seek guidance related to the Act conceming your candidacy for the
city council and the regalar column you write for the local newspaper. You have been a
regular columnist for several years. While, there is no specific subject you address in the
column, you often address local political issues and have expressly addressed your
candidacy for city council within your column dated July 28 to August 17,2006, entitled,
o'Why I'm Running for City Council. You are not paid or compensated in any way for
writing the column.
ANALYSIS
L. Conflict of Interest
Under the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions, writing a column for the local
newspaper does not, in itself, qeate a disquali$ring conflict of interest. Section 87100
prohibits anypublic official from making,participating in making, or otherwise using his
or her official position to influence a governmental decision inwhich the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" itagovernmental decision,
within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have amateial financial effect on one or more of the public official's
economic interests. (Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) The Comrnission has adopted
an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifuing
conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(l)-(g).)3
The Act's conflict-of-interest rules are fact-sensitive and apply to specific pending
governmental decisions. However, your inquiry is a general one, and you have not cited
any specific goveflrmental decision. Accordingly, we can provide only a limited
response to your question regarding possible conflicts of interest.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; regulation 18700(bX1).) A "public official" is "every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency. . . ." (Section
82048.) Your candidacy for city council does not make you a public official. However,
should you be elected to the city council you would then be a public official within the
meaning of the Act and would have a disqualifying conflict of interest in any
3 Enclosed is the Commission fact sheet entitled,"CaflI Vote? Overview of Conflicts Laws,,,
which details all eight steps for your future reference.


File No. I-06-163
Page No. 3
governmental decision, which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
one or more of your economic interests.
Under the Act, there are six kinds of economic interests from which a conflict of
interest may arise. They are defined in section 87103 and regulations 18703-18703.5 and
include:
An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a).)
Any business entity in which a public official is a director, officer, partne4
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d);
regulation 187 03 .1 (b).)
An interest in real property in which a public official has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2.)
Any source of income, including promised income, to the public official which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
871 03(c); regulation 1 8703.3.)
Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $360 or more
within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); regulation 18703.4.)
A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This
is also known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; regulation
18703.s.)
Based upon the facts you have provided, you are not currently a public official.
We trust that this letter and the enclosed Commission fact sheet entitled, "Can I Vote?
Overview of Conflicts Laws," which details the standard eighfstep conflict-of-interest
analysis, will help you identiff potential conflicts of interest should you be elected to the
city council. Moreover, should you have any additional questions regarding the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Act as they relate to a specific govemmental decision, if
elected to the city council, it is advisable that you seek further assistance providing all
relevant facts and a description of the govefirmental decision in which you believe you
might have a conflict of interest.
2. Contribution
A "contribution" is defined as any "payment, forgiveness of a loan, payment of a
loan by a third party, or enforceable promise to make a payment, except to the extent full
and an adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from the surrounding
a
o
a
a
o
a


File No. I-06-163
Page No. 4
circumstances that it is not made for political purposes. (Section 82015; regulation
18215.) A payment made atthe behest of a candidate is a conhibution to the candidate
unless full and adequate consideration is received from the candidate or it is clear from
the surrounding circumstances that the payment was made for purposes unrelated to the
candidate's candidacy for elected office. (Section 82}l5;regulation lg2ls.)
AccordinglS where you receive space in a newspaper free of charge, it is
presumed to be an in-kind contribution made by the newspaper unless it is received for
personal pu{poses unrelated to your candidacy for elected office. However, a payment is
related to a candidate's candidacy for office if all or a portion of the payment is used for
election-related activities. (Section 82015(b)@(C).) Section 82015(b)Q)(C) provides a
nonexclusive list of election-related activities, which includes the following:
"(i) Communications that contain express advocacy of the
nomination or election of the candidate or the defeat of his or her
opponent.
(ii) Communications that contain reference to the candidate,s
candidacy for elective office, the candidate's election campaign, or the
candidate's or his or her opponent's qualifications for elective office.
(iii) Solicitation of contributions to the candidate or to third
persons for use in support of the candidate or in opposition to his or her
opponent.
(iv) Arranging, coordinating, developing, writing, distributing,
preparing, or planning of any communication or activity described in
clauses (i), (ii), or (iii), above.
(v) Recruiting or coordinating campaign activities of campaign
volunteers on behalf of the candidate.
(vi) Preparing campaign budgets
(vii) Preparing campaign finance disclosure statements.
(viii) Communications directed to voters or potential voters as part
of activities encouraging or assisting persons to vote if the communication
contains express advocacy of the nomination or election of the candidate
or the defeat of his or her opponent."
You state that you regularly deal with local political issues in your column. In
addition to addressing local political issues, your column has also expressly addressed
your candidacy for city council. We have previously advised that were a column


File No. I-06-163
Page No. 5
contains express advocacy, refers to the candidate's candidacy or qualifications, refers to
the qualifications of the candidate's opponent, or solicits contribution, the free publishing
of the column would be considered a reportable contribution to the candidat e.a- (Parist
Advice Letter, No. A-98-269.)
Your column is an election-related activity if it contains express advocacy, refers
to your candidacy or qualifications, refers to your opponent's qualifications, or solicits
contributions. If your column is an election-related activity, the newspaper's payment of
the cost of publishing your column is not for personal purposes but for purposes related
to your candidacy for elected office, and the publishing of your column in the newspaper
should be treated as an in-kind contribution and reported accordingly.s
Your column dated July 28 to August 11,2006, entitled, "Why I'm Running for
City Council," is clearly an election-related activity as it expressly references your
candidacy for office. Consequently, the publishing of this column is related to your
candidacy for office and should be considered a reportable in-kind contribution, unless
the newspaper offers equal opportunity and treatment to all other candidates for the same
office as described below.
Notwithstanding a conclusion that the publishing of your column, containing
references to your candidacy for office, in the local newspaper should be treated as an in-
kind contribution, discounts or rebates provided by a television station, radio station, or
newspaper are not contributions if the discount is or rebate is extended on an equal basis
to all candidates for the same office. (Section 82015.)
Accordingly,the publishing of your column, containing references to your
candidacy for office, by the local newspaper is not considered a contribution if the
newspaper offers equal opportunity and treatment to all other candidates for the same
office. We note that it is not necessary that the other candidates actually publish a
column in the newspaper. Section 82015 only requires that the publisher of the
newspaper offer the free space on the same terms and conditions to other candidates as it
is offered to you and provide free space to those who wish to accept the offer. (Burns
Advice Letter, No. I-91-529.)
4 "Express advocact''is a communication that contains express words of advocacy such as "vote
for," "electr" "supportr" "cast your ballot," "vote againstr" "defeat," "rejectr" "sign petitions forr" or
otherwise refers to a clearly identified candidate or measure so that the communication, taken as a whole,
unambiguously urges a particular result in an election. @egulation 18225(b)(2).)
5 The Act requires candidates and recipient committees to report conhibutions received and
expenditures made on periodic campaign statements. (Sectiou 84200.) Should you have additional
questions related to the reporting of contributions and expenditures, it is advisable that you seek further
assistance providing all relevant facts.


File No. I-06-163
Page No. 6
If you have other questions on this matter,please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
By:
Enclosures

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you, a candidate for city
council, from writing a column for the local newspaper?
2. Is the publication of your column by a newspaper without any charge to you a
reportable contribution to you from the newspaper?

CONCLUSION: 1. The Act does not prohibit you from writing a column for the local newspaper
during your candidacy for office. If elected, writing the column without compensation
does not, in itself violate the Act's conflict-of-interest rules.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83 I 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
2. The free publication of your column containing references to your candidacy
by the local newspaper may be a reportable contribution unless the newspaper offers
equal opporfunity and treatment for all candidates for the same office.
